## Stage: facade owns topology

In progress at 0.75.0
([chores](chores/chores-14.md#refactor-facade-owns-topology)).
Repo resolution becomes facade state, not per-command
preludes.

- `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` por equalization: replace the
  `other_repo_from_config` prelude in both
  (`validate_desc.rs:133`, `fix_desc.rs:152`) with
  scope-aware resolution that no-ops `Side::Bot` when
  absent; body unchanged. The 0.61.0 audit [[2]] called this
  the cheapest equalization and the prototype for the
  topology-from-config rule (topology from `default_scope`,
  not a flag); the remaining implementation gaps live in
  [[2]]'s `## Gap list`.
- Make the bot repo directory name configurable (not
  hardcoded `.claude`; decided 2026-07-15 the name need not
  be `.claude`; decided 2026-07-21 the new default is
  `.bot`). The literal is embedded across
  init/clone/push/sync, `claude_path()`, diagnostic /
  logging strings (`bm-track`'s `.claude(main)=no-jj`),
  ochid path semantics (`/.claude/<chid>`), the
  `~/.claude/projects/<path>` symlink, and docs;
  `.vc-config.toml` already records workspace paths — the
  natural home. Schema shape decided 2026-07-23: the
  symmetric `work = "/"` / `bot = "/.bot"` pair *replaces*
  `path`/`other-repo` (identical `[workspace]` block on both
  sides — nothing to cross-check); side detection by
  location, walking up from cwd via `find_workspace_root`
  (root → work, `<root>/<bot-dir>` → bot; was the deferred
  half of `0.41.1-6`'s title; design in
  [chores-08 Operations](chores/chores-08.md#operations));
  presence of `bot` is the dual-repo signal (replacing
  presence-of-`other-repo`); POR is `work = "/"` alone.
  - Migration decided 2026-07-23: none — the config records
    each workspace's actual bot dir, so existing workspaces
    keep `.claude` (`bot = "/.claude"`) and their
    `/.claude/<chid>` trailers keep resolving verbatim;
    `.bot` is the *new-init default only* — a
    recommendation, not a mandate.
  - Still open: the harness-controlled symlink location,
    and the harness's own `<project>/.claude`
    settings/skills dir — renaming the bot repo splits it
    from the vendor's dir (only bites a workspace that
    *chooses* a non-`.claude` dir).
- `config` command reshape (decided 2026-07-23 during the
  0.75.0-2 review): positional `work|bot|work,bot|<path>`
  target (default `work,bot`), print by default with
  `--validate` switching to check mode; validate covers both
  sides' files plus the identical-`[workspace]`-block
  invariant; `--home` retires; the user-home file stays
  load-bearing but is reachable only via an explicit
  `<path>`.
- Path grammar for `work` / `bot` (pinned 2026-07-23): ochid
  path syntax — a leading `/` is *the workspace root*
  (workspace-relative, never filesystem-absolute). `work` is
  exactly `"/"`; `bot` is `"/" + one path component`, no
  trailing slash (the bot repo sits directly under the root —
  side detection and the root walk assume it). The `bot`
  value doubles verbatim as the bot-side ochid prefix.
  Defined and *enforced* at 0.75.0-3 (pulled forward from the
  `-4` plan — no downside): every resolver rejects a
  non-`"/"` `work` or a malformed `bot` loudly, since either
  would otherwise be silently ignored or corrupt trailers.
  The `-4` `config --validate` reuses the same check as the
  human-facing report.
- Dual-mode preflight principle (decided 2026-07-23, "my
  opinion ATM"): an operation entering dual-repo mode
  verifies both repos' expected state up front — bot dir
  exists, both configs parse, `[workspace]` blocks identical
  — and on any finding it stops, prints everything known
  (both paths, both blocks), and changes nothing. Mid-flight
  surprises remain per-operation concerns (sync's
  stop-and-inspect, push's rollback); *entry* is gated at
  the one topology resolver the 0.75.0-3 sweep centralizes.
  The full every-invocation form (validate-bot everywhere)
  stays a separate Todo — it has per-invocation cost
  questions.
- Bisect note: the two repos rewind independently — checking
  out a pre-0.75.0-2 work commit pairs an old binary + old
  work config with the bot repo's *current* (new-form)
  config, so bot-side lookups fail with "missing
  workspace.path" (loud but cryptic; old binaries are
  immutable, this paragraph is the decoder). Within either
  repo every commit is functional — schema flips land
  atomically with the configs they read (the 0.75.0-2
  shape), and the per-rung cargo cycle keeps intentionally
  broken commits out of history.

## Stage: de-gitify init

  Defined and *enforced* at 0.75.0-3 (pulled forward from the
  `-4` plan — no downside): every resolver rejects a
  non-`"/"` `work` or a malformed `bot` loudly, since either
  would otherwise be silently ignored or corrupt trailers.
  The `-4` `config --validate` reuses the same check as the
  human-facing report.
- Dual-mode preflight principle (decided 2026-07-23, "my
  opinion ATM"): an operation entering dual-repo mode
  verifies both repos' expected state up front — bot dir
  exists, both configs parse, `[workspace]` blocks identical
  — and on any finding it stops, prints everything known
  (both paths, both blocks), and changes nothing. Mid-flight
  surprises remain per-operation concerns (sync's
  stop-and-inspect, push's rollback); *entry* is gated at
  the one topology resolver the 0.75.0-3 sweep centralizes.
  The full every-invocation form (validate-bot everywhere)
  stays a separate Todo — it has per-invocation cost
  questions.
- Bisect note: the two repos rewind independently — checking
  out a pre-0.75.0-2 work commit pairs an old binary + old
  work config with the bot repo's *current* (new-form)
  config, so bot-side lookups fail with "missing
  workspace.path" (loud but cryptic; old binaries are
  immutable, this paragraph is the decoder). Within either
  repo every commit is functional — schema flips land
  atomically with the configs they read (the 0.75.0-2
  shape), and the per-rung cargo cycle keeps intentionally
  broken commits out of history.

content.content:
## refactor: facade owns topology

Commits: see [As-built ladder](#as-built-ladder-2)

Repo resolution is a per-command prelude (each command
re-derives "where is the other repo" from
`path`/`other-repo`); it becomes facade state. Third stage of
the refactor program. Decisions at cycle open (2026-07-23):

- `.vc-config.toml` `[workspace]` adopts the symmetric
  `work = "/"` / `bot = "/.bot"` pair, *replacing*
  `path`/`other-repo` — the block is identical on both
  sides, so there is nothing to keep mutually consistent;
  side detection comes from location (walk up from cwd via
  `find_workspace_root`: at the root → work side, at
  `<root>/<bot-dir>` → bot side).
- Presence of `bot` is the dual-repo signal; POR is
  `work = "/"` alone. One vocabulary across surfaces:
  `-s work,bot`, config `work`/`bot`, `Side::Work`/`Side::Bot`.
- `.bot` is the new-init default only — a recommendation.
  Existing workspaces keep their recorded dir (vc-x1 stays
  `bot = "/.claude"`), old `/.claude/<chid>` trailers keep
  resolving verbatim; no migration.

### As-built ladder

- [[N]] 0.75.0-0 chore: open facade owns topology cycle
  - version 0.75.0-0; stage picked into `## In Progress`
    with the five-rung ladder; this section opened; stage
    decisions recorded here, in the ladder block, and in the
    stage section of
    [refactor-20260716.md](../refactor-20260716.md#stage-facade-owns-topology)
  - rider: 0.74.0 ladder-ref backfill (`-3` + close-out
    rungs → [[12]],[[13]])
  - rider: `## Done` retirement sweep — the 0.69.1–0.71.0
    batch (eight entries) migrated to done.md; TODO.md
    references re-packed to `[1]..[5]`
  - rider: the DRY facade and hygiene stages' shipped-status
    links verified in the refactor doc (added at 0.74.0)
- [[N]] 0.75.0-1 refactor: topology por equalization
  - `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` lose their dual-required
    `other_repo_from_config` prelude: a single-repo / POR
    workspace now no-ops with a clear message instead of
    erroring; a dual workspace resolves as before, and
    `--other-repo` still overrides
  - the replacement is `common::bot_repo_path(root)` —
    `default_scope` gates (has the workspace a bot side?),
    `scope_to_repos` resolves; `Ok(None)` is the no-op
    signal. This is the prototype of the
    topology-from-config rule the rest of the stage extends.
  - `desc_helpers::other_repo_from_config` deleted — the
    helper's last two callers were these preludes
  - tests: three `bot_repo_path` units (dual / single-repo /
    no-config) + a `FixturePor` no-op end-to-end per command
- [[N]] 0.75.0-2 feat: topology work/bot config schema
  - `.vc-config.toml` `[workspace]` flips to the symmetric
    `work = "/"` / `bot = "/.claude"` pair; `path` /
    `other-repo` are gone (unreleased — replaced outright,
    like the scope keyword). init emits one identical dual
    block for both sides (`ConfigRole::{Work,Bot}` collapse
    into `Dual`); this workspace's two committed configs
    flipped in the same commit (the running binary is
    installed before the push that uses them)
  - side detection becomes location-based:
    `common::is_bot_dir(dir)` — true iff the parent dir's
    config names `dir` as its `bot`. `find_workspace_root`
    keys on the `work` key and steps up when standing in the
    bot repo; `sync::is_bot_repo` and the new
    `desc_helpers::ochid_prefix_for(repo)` (replacing the
    content-based `ochid_prefix_from_config`) both ride it
  - `default_scope` / `scope_to_repos` read `bot` (presence =
    dual signal; value is root-relative with leading `/`)
  - legacy files error fast with the solution (decided
    2026-07-23 mid-rung): a config with `path`/`other-repo`
    but no `work` is still *found* by the root walk, and
    every resolver rejects it via
    `common::reject_legacy_config` — the message shows the
    replacement block. Silent degradation to POR was the
    alternative and a foot-gun. A file with both old and new
    keys passes (new keys drive; `config --validate` flags
    the strays as unknown).
  - riders: README caught up — the stale "historical
    wrinkle" paragraph (scope keyword renamed at 0.74.0-2),
    `-s code` examples, `remote-code`/`remote-claude`
    fixture names (missed by the 0.74.0-1 sweep), and the
    layout/schema snippets; AGENTS.md `.vc-config.toml`
    section rewritten to the symmetric form
  - incident while landing this rung: the Bugs #3 lock race
    fired at bookmark-set, and the `--yes` rerun resumed
    from the stale state stage, skipping the commit stages —
    session data squashed into the published `-1` bot
    commit. Recorded as Bugs #5 (resume-after-rollback
    replays from the wrong stage); no data loss, and the
    strongest evidence yet for the stateless-push stage
- [[N]] 0.75.0-3 refactor: topology bot-dir sweep
  - every reader of the bot-repo location now resolves it
    from `[workspace] bot`; `.claude` as a *choice* survives
    only in init's `DEFAULT_BOT_DIR` (plus the `Dual` render
    / `GITIGNORE_CODE` literals its doc comment binds to it)
    and the remote-name suffix, which belongs to the
    deferred `.bot`-flip decision set
  - swept: push's `bot_path` (now `require_bot_dir`) and its
    commit-work ochid prefix (via `ochid_prefix_for`),
    `repo_utils::cross_ref_ochids` (prefix from the created
    dir's name), `bm_track`'s probe + label, clone's local
    destination (from the *cloned* work repo's config),
    `validate-bot`'s `-R` default, `symlink`'s default
    target (both fall back to `.claude` when unresolvable)
  - dual-mode entry preflight (the 2026-07-23 principle):
    `bot_repo_path` verifies coherence before anything acts —
    bot dir exists, its config loads, and the two sides'
    `[workspace]` blocks are identical — erroring with both
    paths and both blocks, changing nothing.
    `configured_bot_dir` is the pure-config-read half for
    pre-existence callers (clone)
  - field report, same day: a `vc-x1 push` in another
    workspace (you.h2hist) hit the legacy-config error from
    `-2`; the bot there applied the fix printed in the
    message and the push went through — the
    error-fast-with-the-solution loop working as designed
  - path grammar pinned *and enforced* (pulled forward from
    the `-4` plan during review — no downside):
    `check_workspace_grammar` at the same resolver
    chokepoints rejects `work` ≠ `"/"` (a name tag every
    reader ignores — the last silent-lie case) and a `bot`
    that isn't `/` + one component (an unanchored value
    corrupts ochid trailers)
  - stage-doc prose riders: the grammar definition, the
    dual-preflight principle, and the bisect-skew note
    (the two repos rewind independently; old binaries fail
    loudly-but-cryptically against the new bot-side config)

### clone

Clone an existing dual-repo project. Runs `git clone --recursive` to
get both repos, initializes `jj` in each, and creates the Claude Code
symlink.

```
# Clone using GitHub shorthand
vc-x1 clone owner/my-project

# Clone using full URL
vc-x1 clone git@github.com:owner/my-project.git

# Clone with a custom directory name
vc-x1 clone owner/my-project my-local-name

# Clone into a specific parent directory
vc-x1 clone owner/my-project --dir ~/projects

# Preview without executing
vc-x1 clone owner/my-project --dry-run
```

### config

Print every settable config key across vc-x1's two config
homes — the user config (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`) and the
workspace config (`<root>/.vc-config.toml`) — as an annotated,
commented schema: for each key its description, the
command/context it's `used by`, its default (or a value marked
`# example` when there is no default), and a commented
assignment line ready to paste in. The schema is generated from
one in-code registry, so it can't drift from what the code
actually reads.

- `--home user|workspace|all` — filter to one home's keys
  [default: all].
- `--validate` — instead of printing, load the actual config
  file(s) for the selected home(s) and flag any key the schema
  doesn't recognize (a typo, a key in the wrong section, or an
  unknown key). Exits non-zero if any are found. This is an
  opt-in strict check — a normal config load silently ignores
  unknown keys, for forward-compatibility.

A short sample of the printed schema (workspace home):

```
[bot-session]

content.text:
No code change pending on either point — both are explanation:

**`--scope` isn't on `config`, by design.** `config` never grew a `--scope` flag; the positional `[TARGET]` *reuses the same keyword vocabulary* (`work`, `bot`, `work,bot`, `bot,work` — literally parsed by the same `parse_scope` function), but it's a positional argument, not the flag. `--scope`/`-s` lives on the commit-query commands (`chid`, `desc`, `list`, `show`), where it selects which repos to query. When I said "parsed with the `--scope` keyword grammar" in the summary, I meant the shared vocabulary, not that the flag exists here — sorry for the confusion. Nothing is deferred to close-out.

**"User or Workspace" are the printed group titles**, and they relate to what `[TARGET]` resolves to. The schema print opens each group with a divider line, and which groups you get follows from the target:

- `work` → one `# ── Work config: <root>/.vc-config.toml ──` group (keys settable on the work side).
- `bot` → one `# ── Bot config: <root>/.claude/.vc-config.toml ──` group.
- `work,bot` (the default) → both of those.
- `<path>` that names the user config file → one `# ── User config: <path> ──` group (the user-only keys like `[default]`, `[repo]`).
- any other `<path>` → one `# ── Workspace config: <path> ──` group — since a bare path doesn't say which side the file is, it accepts either side's keys.

So "User or Workspace" was describing the two cases for a *path* target specifically: user-config path gets the User key set, everything else gets the union workspace key set. You can see it directly with `vc-x1-dev config ~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` versus plain `vc-x1-dev config`.

The plain version: the keywords in `[TARGET]` are the same words `--scope` uses elsewhere, but here they're the positional argument — there's no missing flag. And "User/Workspace/Work/Bot" are just the section headers the printout uses, chosen by what you passed as the target.

content.text:
Revised and green — the User/Workspace guessing is gone. What changed:

- **Path target = whole schema, no sniffing.** `is_user_config` (the canonicalize-and-compare detection, including the flagged `unwrap_or_else`) is deleted, along with the `in_user`/`in_workspace` predicates. A path target now prints and validates against the full key registry via a trivial `in_any`, since a bare path carries no side information.
- **Dividers use the target vocabulary.** Keyword groups print `# ── work: <resolved path> ──` / `# ── bot: <resolved path> ──`; a path target's divider is just the path itself. "User config" / "Workspace config" labels are gone.
- **Validation split is now exact:** keyword targets get side-filtered keys plus the `[workspace]` grammar and coherence checks; a path target gets a pure unknown-key check against the whole schema. (The legacy `path`/`other-repo` keys aren't in the schema, so a legacy file passed by path is still flagged — as unknown keys.)
- Docs and tests follow: module doc, README config section, the chores rung records the decision, and the CLI tests pin the new dividers (`cli_config_user_path` now asserts the whole schema shows, `[default]` *and* `[workspace]`).

Full cycle green again: fmt, clippy `-D warnings`, all 515 tests, install (`vc-x1-dev 0.75.0-4`). Ready for your review.

You're mostly right, and I'll try to be precise about where the one real trap is. Let me take the pieces in order.

**On the word "workspace":** agreed it's overloaded — Cargo workspaces, VS Code workspaces, jj workspaces (jj literally has `jj workspace`, which is the worst collision since we're a jj tool). Since the schema is unreleased-in-practice (we just replaced it wholesale at -2), renaming the section is cheap *now* and only gets more expensive.

**On the current `/`-anchored grammar:** it bought exactly three things, and it's worth being explicit because they're what has to be replaced:

- The **literal identical-block invariant** — both sides carry the same bytes because values are anchored to a shared origin, not to the file's own location. File-relative paths are fundamentally incompatible with literal identity: `bot = ".claude"` means the right thing in the root config and the wrong thing in the bot config. So dropping the anchor means replacing "identical text" with "**resolved agreement**" — canonicalize both sides' pairs and check they name the same two directories. Honestly, that's a *stronger* check (it compares reality, not spelling), and the coherence preflight from -3 already has the machinery.
- **Side detection by nesting** — root walk + "am I the parent's declared bot dir". With file-relative paths this gets simpler, not harder: whichever entry resolves to the config's own directory is your side (`work = "."` → I'm the work repo).
- **The verbatim ochid trailer prefix** — and this is where your URL point does the heavy lifting. Trailers are *published, clone-portable* text. Root-relative prefixes were only portable because nesting made the pair travel as a unit. The moment repos need not be nested, no filesystem spelling in a trailer can be portable — so the trailer prefix must become an opaque repo *label* (today `/`, `/.claude`; later a URL) that the local config maps to a path. That reframes the section from "layout description" to "repo registry": stable name → local path now, → URL later. Your URL intention isn't a complication for this design; it's the completion of it.

**Your proposed path semantics — I'd pin them as:** a value is an ordinary path, either absolute or relative to *the directory containing the config file that states it*. That's the standard, unsurprising rule (git's `includeIf`, cargo path-deps, systemd — everything file-relative works this way), and it has none of the "leading `/` secretly means something else" surprise.

**Where you might be wrong — the one real trap:** the committed-file portability problem. Today, `bot = "/.claude"` committed in the repo is portable *because* nesting guarantees every clone reproduces the layout. Once you allow `bot = "../foo-bot"` or an absolute path in a **committed** file, you've committed *the author's machine layout* — the next clone puts the repos wherever it likes and the committed path is a lie. So dropping the anchor doesn't just relax the grammar; it eventually forces a split between **committed identity** (name/URL — true in every clone) and **local layout** (paths — true on this machine, belonging in an uncommitted local file, the way `.git/config` holds remotes). Nested layouts can keep committed relative paths honestly; non-nested ones can't. That's not an argument against your direction — it's the same conclusion as the URL move, arrived at from the other side — but it means the change is bigger than a grammar swap.

**My recommendation:** agree — drop the `/`-anchored pseudo-paths, adopt file-relative + absolute with resolved-agreement replacing identical-blocks, and treat trailer prefixes as labels resolved through config. But that's a stage of its own (it touches side detection, coherence, ochid prefixing, clone, init, symlink, and opens the committed-vs-local split), not a -4 rework. I'd land -4 as reviewed, and capture this as a pinned decision + new Todo/stage entry ("repo registry: file-relative paths, label-based ochids") so 0.75.0 closes on the scheme it opened with. Want me to draft that entry?

The plain version: your instinct is right — plain relative-and-absolute paths are the precise, standard choice, and the special "/" syntax should go. The one thing to watch: a committed config can only honestly contain a relative path if the two repos always travel together (nested); once they can live anywhere, the committed file should carry names or URLs, and each machine keeps its own private note of where the repos actually sit. That's exactly where your ochid-as-URL idea was already heading.

## Stage: repo registry

Decided 2026-07-24 (during the 0.75.0-4 review). The
`[workspace]` block becomes a repo registry — stable names
mapped to locations — and the root-anchored path grammar
(leading `/` = workspace root, pinned at 0.75.0-3) is
dropped. Complements the URL-shaped-ochid design in
[forks-multi-user.md](forks-multi-user.md#per-user-bot-repos-via-url-shaped-ochid)
(the "ochid: bot-repo location qualifier" Todo): that entry
owns the published/URL half; this stage owns the local-path
half.

- Rename the section: "workspace" is overloaded (cargo,
  VS Code, and jj itself has `jj workspace`) — vc-x1 is
  medium-agnostic. Candidate `[repos]`; settle at pickup.
- Path semantics for `work` / `bot`: an ordinary path —
  absolute, or relative to the directory containing the
  config file that states it (the standard file-relative
  rule; no leading-`/`-means-root special case). Docs
  recommend relative paths; absolute paths are allowed but
  discouraged — they commit one machine's layout.
- The literal identical-block invariant cannot survive
  file-relative values (the same bytes mean different dirs
  in each file); it is replaced by **resolved agreement** —
  canonicalize each side's declared pair and require both
  files to name the same two directories. The 0.75.0-3
  coherence preflight keeps its role, comparing reality
  instead of spelling.
- Side detection: the entry that resolves to the config's
  own directory names the side (`work = "."` → work repo).
  Repos need not be nested.
- ochid trailer prefixes become opaque repo labels resolved
  through the registry, not filesystem spellings: `/` and
  `/.claude` remain valid as historical labels for
  work/bot, so published trailers keep resolving; labels
  are intended to grow into URLs (the forks-multi-user
  design), making trailers clone-portable with no layout
  assumption.
- Committed identity vs local layout: a committed relative
  path is only honest while the repos travel together
  (nested, or siblings under a committed root). Non-nested
  layouts need the registry split — committed name/URL,
  per-machine local path in an uncommitted local file —
  designed here even if URL support lands later.

We think this should run immediately after facade owns
topology (before de-gitify init): the schema is unreleased
and freshly adopted (one external workspace migrated at
0.75.0-2), so moving now means one migration wave instead
of two, and the init/clone rework (de-gitify init, the
template restructure) lands on the final schema instead of
being rewritten twice.

## Stage: de-gitify init

content.text:
Todo added and the list renumbered. My suggestion, now in place: **rank #2 — the very next cycle after 0.75.0 closes**, ahead of "de-gitify init". The reasoning:

- The schema is unreleased and freshly adopted — h2hist migrated once at 0.75.0-2. Doing the registry change immediately means external workspaces see **one migration wave, not two**.
- De-gitify init (#3) and the template restructure (#9) both rework init/clone; they should be built on the final schema rather than rewritten after it changes.
- The rest of the program (split push.rs, stateless push, jj-lib) doesn't touch the topology surface, so nothing else is blocked by slotting this in first.

What I recorded:

- **`notes/refactor-20260716.md`** — a new "Stage: repo registry" section between facade-owns-topology and de-gitify init, pinning today's decisions: section rename (candidate `[repos]`; "workspace" collides with `jj workspace` of all things), file-relative + absolute path semantics with docs recommending relative and discouraging-but-allowing absolute, resolved agreement replacing the identical-block invariant, side detection by which entry resolves to `.`, ochid prefixes as registry labels with `/` and `/.claude` kept as historical labels, and the committed-identity vs local-layout split for non-nested repos. It cross-links forks-multi-user.md — the existing "ochid: bot-repo location qualifier" Todo (#10 after renumbering) keeps the published/URL half; this stage owns the local-path half.
- **`TODO.md`** — the entry at #2 summarizing the above; `fix-todo` renumbered the 18 entries below it.

These notes edits will ride in the -4 commit along with everything else. The -4 work itself is still sitting at your review from before this discussion — let me know when you're happy with it and I'll draft the commit description.

Here's the proposed commit description:

**Title:**
```
feat: topology config target
```

**Body:**
```
The config command reshape decided at the -2 review: a positional
work|bot|work,bot|<path> target replaces --home, and --validate
grows the topology invariants. Riders: the vc-x1-dev package
rename and the repo-registry stage decision from this rung's
review.

- src/config_cmd.rs
  - positional target: --scope keywords or a config-file path
    (default work,bot); HomeFilter / --home removed
  - print emits one side-filtered group per keyword with
    `# ── work: <path> ──` dividers; a path target gets the
    whole schema under a plain-path divider (a path carries no
    side information — nothing is guessed from it)
  - --validate covers unknown keys plus, for keyword targets,
    the [workspace] grammar (reject_legacy_config) and the
    identical-block coherence check (riding bot_repo_path);
    failures count as findings so the work-side report still
    lands; 12 new unit tests
- tests/cli_config.rs: six subprocess tests rewritten for the
  target surface (new dividers, path = whole schema, --home
  cases dropped)
- README.md: config section rewritten for the target grammar
- Cargo.toml: package renamed vc-x1-dev, version 0.75.0-4 —
  dev installs stop clobbering the vc-x1 binary other
  workspaces run; promotion to plain vc-x1 is an explicit act
- Cargo.lock: follows the rename and bump
- notes/versioning.md: new "Dev artifact name" section
- notes/refactor-20260716.md: new "Stage: repo registry"
  section — file-relative/absolute paths, resolved agreement
  replacing identical blocks, ochid prefixes as registry
  labels
- TODO.md: -4 rung marked done; "Refactor stage: repo
  registry" entry added at #2, list renumbered
- notes/chores/chores-14.md: -4 As-built rung (reshape,
  whole-schema path decision, rename rider)
```

The `ochid:` trailer will be injected by `vc-x1 push`. OK to commit — and is this an interim push or shall I just `jj commit` locally and wait for the close-out?
Pushing 0.75.0-4 to `refactor-vc-x1` now — after this lands I'll stop until you direct otherwise.
